issues.title: Improve drag & drop support in the blocks engine

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
# Improve drag & drop support in the blocks engine

## What this issue is about
This issue aims to enhance the drag-and-drop functionality within the Volto blocks engine by transitioning from the `react-beautiful-dnd` library to the actively maintained `react-aria` library. The goal is to facilitate smoother movement of blocks not just within the main blocks stream but also between different block containers.

## What needs to be done
1. Create a central drag-and-drop context for all elements in the blocks engine.
2. Refactor specific components (`BlocksForm`, `EditBlockWrapper`) to use new drag-and-drop hooks.
3. Develop a new `DragDropList` component using the `dnd-kit` library as a replacement.
4. Improve collision detection and animations for better user experience.
5. Explore alternative visual indicators for dragging, making UX simpler and steadier.

## Helpful background
- The current drag-and-drop setup relies on an outdated library (`react-beautiful-dnd`), making it challenging to implement functional improvements.
- The discussion includes suggestions for a "virtualized list" for better performance on large pages, though this has not been prioritized.

## Good first steps
1. Review the existing [proof of concept PR](https://github.com/plone/volto/pull/3547) to understand the current progress.
2. Familiarize yourself with the `react-aria` and `dnd-kit` libraries to better understand their usage.
3. Join the conversation in this issue and ask questions if any parts are unclear; community support is available!

issues.title: Create Plone site administrator guide

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
```markdown
## What this issue is about
This issue aims to create a comprehensive guide for Plone site administrators. It will cover navigation through control panels and details on installing add-ons. The guide should include screenshots where possible, according to related implementation plans (PLIP).

## What needs to be done
- Draft the content for the Administrator guide, ensuring it clearly explains how to navigate control panels and install add-ons.
- Consider incorporating a section on creating custom control panels.
- Reconcile and integrate content with any relevant existing discussions or documents, particularly with issue #1768.

## Helpful background
Contributors should familiarize themselves with Plone's existing documentation and the ongoing discussions about the Admin Guide, especially in relation to merging topics with the Developer Guide. There may be some confusion in the requirements and direction, so clarity on the structure is essential.

## Good first steps
1. Review the existing contributing guidelines in the repository.
2. Explore the Plone site to get hands-on experience as an administrator.
3. Check out relevant issues and discussions for context, especially issues #3987, #1768, and #4131.
4. Start drafting initial sections of the guide and share your progress for feedback!
```

issues.title: z3c.form fields with a default value cannot be set to None or ""

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
## What this issue is about
This issue addresses a bug where `z3c.form` fields with a default value can't be set to `None` or an empty string `("")`. When users attempt to clear a field and save it, the default value is incorrectly restored upon re-editing the form.

## What needs to be done
We need to investigate and fix the underlying issue in `z3c.form` that causes default values to be reapplied even when users want to clear fields. This is currently recognized as a bug affecting multiple scenarios in Plone related to custom content types.

## Helpful background
The problem has been noted in various forms, affecting both querying behavior and fields such as `TextLine`. Members of the community have managed to identify that the issue arises with certain versions of `z3c.form`—versions 3.2.11 and later currently exhibit this bug, while 3.2.9 does not. However, there may be additional complexities not yet fully understood.

## Good first steps
1. Review the existing comments and check out the discussed versions of `z3c.form` to understand which changes led to the bug.
2. Test the implementation with `z3c.form` version 3.2.9 to confirm it behaves as expected.
3. Collaborate with other contributors for insights on the code and potential fixes. 
4. Document any findings in your review process for clarity and future reference.

issues.title: PLIP: Keyword Manager

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
## What this issue is about
This issue focuses on implementing a **Keyword Manager** for Plone in the Volto framework. The purpose is to create a tool for managing keywords, enhancing user experience when categorizing and searching content.

## What needs to be done
Develop the base functionality of the **Products.PloneKeywordManager**. Discussions have included possible integrations into bulk actions for folders. The implementation will be done first as an add-on to support a client's needs before moving it into the core application.

## Helpful background
A keyword manager helps users manage keywords efficiently, especially in environments with many keywords. Discussion around the UX and integration suggests there are varying perspectives on features, such as a "show similar keywords" option. Feedback and user experience insights are still being gathered.

## Good first steps
1. **Familiarize yourself** with Plone and the Volto framework.
2. Review the existing discussions and comments for important context.
3. Start by developing a prototype based on the low-fidelity UX design shared in the comments.
4. Coordinate with contributors already involved, like Timo Stollenwerk, to gain insights and establish collaboration.

issues.title: Plone 7 frontend (Seven)

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
```markdown
## What this issue is about
This issue focuses on modernizing the Plone frontend to create a new version called "Seven" that embraces current web development standards, specifically using React Router 7. The goal is to replace outdated libraries and establish a more efficient and flexible architecture for the Plone CMS.

## What needs to be done
The plan includes significant updates to the existing Volto framework, such as:
- Transitioning to a modern bundler, Vite.
- Creating a pluggable standalone app for the CMS user interface.
- Refactoring components and introducing a new theming system without Semantic UI.
- Enhancing the overall content editing experience and ensuring compatibility with existing sites.

## Helpful background
This proposal is part of the Plone Improvement Proposal (PLIP) format. The discussion around this issue has included various perspectives on the direction of the project. Some responses are still ongoing and involve clarifications regarding the "headless" nature of the future setup.

## Good first steps
1. Familiarize yourself with the existing **Volto** framework and its components.
2. Read through the [Seven PLIP discussion](https://github.com/plone/volto/issues/6638) to get insight into what's already been proposed.
3. Consider contributing by drafting documentation or exploring ways to implement compatibility with current add-ons.
4. Engage in the conversation by asking questions if you’re unclear about specific technical aspects.
```

issues.title: Refactor class components to functional components

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
```markdown
## What this issue is about
This issue focuses on refactoring class-based components in Volto to functional components. This change will facilitate the use of React hooks, allowing for improved functionality and easier future upgrades.

## What needs to be done
You will need to convert defined class components to functional components while maintaining their lifecycle and behavior. It is crucial to provide Cypress acceptance tests for any components that currently lack them. If any component contracts change (such as props), proper documentation is required.

## Helpful background
The transition from class-based to functional components in React allows for better management of state and side effects through hooks. This issue has seen significant work already, but some components remain to be refactored.

**Note:** The current discussion indicates that work on this issue is on hold until the next alpha release of Volto 19, with some PRs already submitted and others pending.

## Good first steps
1. Review the existing PRs related to this issue to understand current progress.
2. Familiarize yourself with the Plone and Volto contribution guidelines, especially around component structure.
3. Consider picking one of the unfactored components to refactor or assist in reviewing existing pull requests.
4. Engage in discussions if you have questions or need clarification on refactoring approaches.
```

issues.title: PLIP: Make Products.CMFEditions a Core Add-on

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
```markdown
## What this issue is about
This issue discusses a proposal to move **Products.CMFEditions** from being a core dependency of **Products.CMFPlone** to a core add-on. Currently, every Plone site has it active, which adds overhead even when versioning is not used. The proposal aims to allow sites that don't need versioning to exclude it without losing functionality.

## What needs to be done
To achieve this, several code changes are required in various packages. Important tasks include:
- Removing hard dependencies on **Products.CMFEditions** from **Products.CMFPlone**.
- Implementing conditional imports and fallback mechanisms to ensure functionality without errors if **CMFEditions** is absent.
- Updating tests to skip assertions that assume **CMFEditions** is installed.

## Helpful background
There's a concern about performance overhead due to **CMFEditions**, especially regarding object creation time. While there's some ambiguity in comments about the exact impact of versioning on all content types, tests and benchmarks hint at significant performance implications for sites using versioning.

## Good first steps
1. **Review the PLIP documentation** to understand the process for contributing changes to Plone.
2. Familiarize yourself with the **Products.CMFPlone** repository structure and where dependencies are declared.
3. Start by making small changes, such as modifying dependencies and implementing conditional import patterns.
4. Join the discussion to clarify any uncertainties and seek guidance from the community.
```

issues.title: Move new CSRF docs from Classic UI to Backend?

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
```markdown
## What this issue is about
This issue explores whether the newly created Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) documentation, currently under "Classic UI," should be moved to "Backend" and updated for Volto. The discussions indicate that CSRF protection may not apply to the frontend but needs clarification in the documentation.

## What needs to be done
1. **Move the CSRF documentation** from Classic UI to Backend.
2. **Update the content** to specify how CSRF is managed in the Volto environment and clarify its application in Plone 6's different frontends.

## Helpful background
Currently, the documentation doesn’t adequately reflect how CSRF protection works with the Plone REST API and various frontends. Ongoing discussions suggest that while CSRF checks are generally not necessary in JSON web services, the framework requires further attention to properly document this.

## Good first steps
1. Review and familiarize yourself with the current CSRF documentation [here](https://6.docs.plone.org/classic-ui/csrf.html).
2. Begin drafting proposed changes to the content based on discussions and insights from contributors.
3. Engage with the team for clarification on any points of confusion, particularly regarding the backend's CSRF protections.
```

issues.title: Create User Manual with screenshots and videos for Plone 6

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
```markdown
## What this issue is about
This issue focuses on creating a comprehensive User Manual for Plone 6, including screenshots and videos. Currently, much of the documentation is outdated, particularly related to the Classic UI. By using automated tools, we can streamline the process of keeping this manual accurate and accessible.

## What needs to be done
1. Investigate tools like Cypress and Playwright that can automate the capturing of screenshots and videos.
2. Port the existing Plone 5 user manual to Plone 6, ensuring it’s in MyST format with SEO headers.
3. Set up automation via GitHub workflows to update these visuals with every pull request.

## Helpful background
Older documentation can be daunting to update continuously. The goal here is to leverage automation to enhance the user experience without overloading users with visuals. Discussions suggest a balance between visuals and narrative text is essential, addressing concerns that too many visuals can clutter documentation.

## Good first steps
1. Explore the existing [Content Editing training guide](https://training.plone.org/content-editing/index.html) for reuse opportunities.
2. Familiarize yourself with Cypress Studio and other mentioned tools.
3. Join the conversation at PloneConf 2025 to discuss the direction and gather feedback.
4. Consider collaborating with others who are interested in this effort.

Feel free to ask questions if anything is unclear!
```

issues.title: Include diazo documentation in Classic backend documentation

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
```markdown
## What this issue is about
This issue seeks to include the Diazo documentation into the Plone documentation repository. Currently, the Diazo documentation is hosted separately at [diazo.org](https://docs.diazo.org/en/latest/), and there is a suggestion to integrate it more closely with Plone’s documentation.

## What needs to be done
1. Find and contact the maintainers of the Diazo documentation on ReadTheDocs to request maintainer access.
2. Convert the existing Diazo documentation to MyST format, which is a format compatible with markdown.
3. Decide where to add these documents in the Plone repository as a Git submodule.
4. Set up the submodule in the `plone/documentation` following [these guidelines](https://6.docs.plone.org/contributing/documentation/admins.html#importing-external-docs-with-submodules).

## Helpful background
Diazo is a tool for theming in Plone, and integrating its documentation could enhance the user experience. The discussions are ongoing, and there might be additional criteria that need clarification.

## Good first steps
1. Familiarize yourself with the Diazo documentation at [diazo.org](https://docs.diazo.org/en/latest/).
2. Check the [Plone documentation contribution guide](https://6.docs.plone.org/contributing/documentation/) for setup instructions.
3. Consider reaching out to the current maintainers for guidance as you begin this work.

Feel free to ask questions if you're unsure about anything!
```

issues.title: Search the control panel to find a setting

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
```markdown
## What this issue is about
This issue proposes adding a search feature in the Plone control panel to allow users to locate specific settings quickly, similar to the search function in macOS's System Settings. The idea is still in the discussion phase, and there are various viewpoints on how to implement it effectively.

## What needs to be done
We need to design a functionality that lets users search for specific settings and see which control panels contain them. There is currently no clear implementation path, and discussions suggest starting with a simple search bar that filters existing settings, eventually moving towards a more advanced solution.

## Helpful background
Contributors have mentioned the importance of both global and filtered search options within the control panel. There is no existing widget that mimics the desired functionality, but notable methods include indexing fields from control panel forms.

## Good first steps
1. Familiarize yourself with the discussions in the issue. 
2. Check the [Contributing Guide for Plone](https://6.docs.plone.org/contributing/index.html) and the [Volto contributions](https://6.docs.plone.org/contributing/volto.html) to understand the contribution process.
3. Consider trying to implement a basic search feature that filters through current settings; additional complexity can be added later.
4. Engage with existing contributors for feedback and guidance throughout the development process.
```

issues.title: Automate screenshots and videos

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
```markdown
## What this issue is about
This issue focuses on enhancing Plone documentation by automating the capture of current screenshots and videos of the user interface for both Classic UI and Volto. The goal is to ensure that whenever there’s a change in the UI, contributors are notified in pull requests or during testing.

## What needs to be done
We need to develop a system that automatically captures screenshots of the Plone interface and detects any changes. Suggestions have included using tools like Cypress and Playwright. A recent tool, Heroshot, has also gained attention for its potential integration with Sphinx documentation.

## Helpful background
Historically, this feature existed in Plone 5 but is now unsupported. There's ongoing exploration of automating this process using modern tools. The discussion includes various contributors testing and proposing ideas, making this a collaborative effort.

## Good first steps
1. **Familiarize yourself** with the tools mentioned, especially Cypress, Playwright, and Heroshot.
2. **Test Heroshot** with Plone documentation by creating a new branch and running initial tests, as suggested by @stevepiercy.
3. **Document your findings** on Heroshot’s usability and report any bugs or enhancement suggestions.
4. Start engaging in discussions on the existing PRs or issues to get acclimated with the project.
```

issues.title: Move videos from the User Manual to a CDN or YouTube

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
## What this issue is about
This issue discusses moving videos from the Plone user manual to an external hosting solution, like a Content Delivery Network (CDN) or YouTube. This is to reduce the repository's size, which currently contains videos that contribute significant disk usage.

## What needs to be done
1. **Audit** existing video assets in the documentation.
2. **Select** a hosting option (CDN or YouTube).
3. **Update** all references to the videos in the documentation.
4. **Document** the recommended approach for future video uploads.

## Helpful background
The current video storage contributes about 8MB to the repository size, which can hinder development. This issue is marked as a complex PLIP (Product Life-cycle Improvement Proposal) task, meaning it requires careful coordination among various teams within Plone, including Documentation, Admin, and Marketing.

## Good first steps
1. **Familiarize yourself** with Plone and its documentation systems.
2. **Join discussions** with other contributors to understand hosting preferences or constraints.
3. **Explore the GitHub link** on removing large files from history to grasp the complexities involved.
4. **Be prepared** for potential coordination efforts if you plan to contribute.

issues.title: Unpin sphinx in requirements.txt

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
## What this issue is about
This issue discusses the need to unpin the Sphinx dependency from `sphinx<9` in the `requirements.txt` file. The current pinning is a temporary solution to address an underlying issue that needs resolution. The ultimate goal is to finalize and release Plone Sphinx Theme v2.0.0, removing the dependency on the Sphinx Book Theme.

## What needs to be done
The primary task is to remove the version restriction for Sphinx in the `requirements.txt` file once the related work on Plone Sphinx Theme v2.0.0 is complete. However, it's important to note that the specifics of what remains to be done and any contributing guidelines are currently unclear.

## Helpful background
Sphinx is a documentation generator, and pinning it helps avoid potential compatibility issues. The Sphinx Book Theme is an additional theme that complicates the current setup, making it essential to address it to move forward.

## Good first steps
1. Familiarize yourself with Sphinx and the Plone Sphinx Theme.
2. Check for current development branches or documentation related to this issue.
3. Engage with existing discussions for context, but be prepared that the requirements may shift as this is a complex area with an ambiguous roadmap. 

Feel free to reach out if you have questions or need guidance!

The specific tasks for this issue are currently unclear and may involve a particular variation of the Listing block. There are indications that the author, [ebrehault](https://github.com/ebrehault), is already working on it, so coordination with them is essential to avoid duplicated effort.

Due to the complexity labeled in this issue, it may not be suitable for first-time contributors. issues.title: [Seven] - Make APISUFFIX configurable via environment variables

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
```markdown
## What this issue is about
This issue focuses on making the API suffix in Volto easily configurable via environment variables. Currently, the default suffix (`/++api++`) is hardcoded, which complicates integration with alternative backends like [Nick](https://nickcms.org) or [Guillotina](https://plone.org/why-plone/plone-ecosystem/guillotina).

## What needs to be done
The goal is to implement a way for developers to set the API suffix through an environment variable (e.g., `PLONE_API_SUFFIX`). This would allow for easier customization and flexibility when using different Plone RestAPI-compatible backends.

## Helpful background
- Now, to use backends that don’t rely on the `++api++` prefix, a workaround is needed, such as defining a custom `PloneClient`. 
- There is an existing variable, `RAZZLE_LEGACY_TRAVERSE`, for similar purposes in Volto but it’s unclear if it fully meets the needs for the `@plone/client`.

## Good first steps
1. Review the current implementation in `@plone/client` to understand how it handles API calls.
2. Explore how `RAZZLE_LEGACY_TRAVERSE` functions as a reference.
3. Create a prototype for setting the API suffix through an environment variable.
4. Make sure to check the [First-Time Contributors Guide](https://6.docs.plone.org/contributing/first-time.html) for guidance on your contributions.
5. Consider documenting your changes and coordinate with other contributors as needed.
```

issues.title: Implement non-schema-driven control panels

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
## What this issue is about
This issue focuses on implementing non-schema-driven control panels in the Plone Volto project. Control panels are settings interfaces that allow users to manage configuration options without being tied to specific data models (schemas). This could enhance flexibility and user customization.

## What needs to be done
You will need to design and implement these control panels to manage different aspects, such as add-ons, users, and groups. Since the issue is open and there is limited detail and no specific guidance in the comments, you might encounter some ambiguity in requirements.

## Helpful background
Understanding control panels in Plone is important for this task. They allow administrators to adjust settings easily. Non-schema-driven panels can provide a more fluid user experience by enabling custom configurations without strict data structures.

## Good first steps
1. **Familiarize yourself with existing control panels** in the Volto codebase to see how they are currently structured.
2. **Review the existing documentation** on Plone and Volto to better understand implementation context.
3. **Reach out to the author, [ksuess](https://github.com/ksuess)**, or the community for clarification on any uncertainties.
4. **Consider drafting a proposal** for your approach before starting implementation, as this could help clarify expectations.

issues.title: Metadata block or easy way to insert document metadata inside text block

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
```markdown
## What this issue is about
This issue focuses on enhancing Volto by introducing a way to insert document metadata into text blocks. The goal is to allow users to easily display metadata, such as publication dates or tags, within content areas. This could greatly improve how dynamic pages are built.

## What needs to be done
The enhancement involves creating a new block type or adding a button in the text block that pulls content from metadata fields. Discussions indicate that integrating this feature into the core would be beneficial, although there are some reservations about adding extra blocks.

## Helpful background
Current discussions mention existing add-ons like [volto-metadata-block](https://github.com/eea/volto-metadata-block) and [volto-slate-metadata-mentions](https://github.com/eea/volto-slate-metadata-mentions), which provide similar features. However, there is a strong sentiment that having metadata functionalities in core would simplify the user experience.

## Good first steps
1. Familiarize yourself with the existing add-ons mentioned for additional context.
2. Review the current implementation of text blocks in Volto.
3. Discuss with other contributors about how best to integrate this feature into the core.
4. Consider starting by drafting a proposal or mockups for the new block/button functionality.
```

issues.title: Add details about each of the default behaviors

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
```markdown
## What this issue is about
This issue involves enhancing the Plone documentation by adding detailed explanations of the default behaviors within the system. Current descriptions may be too brief, and there's discussion about possibly separating these details from the general overview of how to use behaviors.

## What needs to be done
You will need to write comprehensive descriptions for each of the default behaviors in Plone, explaining what they do and their significance. It's important to ensure these explanations are clear and accessible, especially for those new to Plone.

## Helpful background
Behaviors in Plone are a way to extend the functionality of content types. This issue is aimed at improving understanding of these features. However, the discussion indicates that a deep understanding of these behaviors is required, as it can be a complex topic.

## Good first steps
1. Familiarize yourself with Plone's current documentation on behaviors.
2. Read more about contributing to Plone by following [First-time contributors](https://6.docs.plone.org/contributing/first-time.html).
```

issues.title: Create redirects of `docs.plone.org` to `6.docs.plone.org`

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
```markdown
## What this issue is about
This issue focuses on creating 301 redirects from `docs.plone.org` to `6.docs.plone.org` to help guide users and search engines to the updated documentation. It aims to deal with existing links that might still reference the old documentation.

## What needs to be done
We need to set up 301 redirects for various URLs to ensure users are sent to the correct pages on `6.docs.plone.org`. There is also a need to track any resulting 404 errors so we can refine our redirects based on real user data.

## Helpful background
Redirects are essential for maintaining user experience and SEO when content is moved. A 301 redirect signifies that a page has permanently moved to a new location. Current discussions highlight the importance of tracking which URLs generate 404 errors after the redirects are implemented.

## Good first steps
1. Familiarize yourself with the current redirects and the URLs needed.
2. Explore existing discussions for insights on the tracking strategy using Matomo.
3. Consider testing how to generate 404 errors for URLs that aren't redirected, to gather tracking data effectively.
4. Collaborate with the assigned contributors, such as @fredvd and @polyester, for guidance on implementation.
```

issues.title: Create a Cookieplone entry in the Conceptual guide

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
## What this issue is about
This issue focuses on creating a conceptual guide for **Cookieplone** within the Plone documentation. Cookieplone is not well-understood and a detailed guide can help clarify its workflow and tools.

## What needs to be done
1. Create a workflow diagram illustrating the Cookieplone process, from project generation to deployment. You can use **Mermaid** or **Graphviz** for diagrams, and export them as SVG.
2. Provide a description of the tools included in a Cookieplone project, especially those related to DevOps and documentation.
3. Reference existing materials, such as content from [Plone Deployment Training](https://training.plone.org/plone-deployment/).

## Helpful background
The term "conceptual guide" refers to explanations in the **Diátaxis** framework, which outlines how to present complex information clearly. This issue is marked as complex, indicating it may require some experience with Plone.

## Good first steps
- Familiarize yourself with the [Plone Documentation](https://6.docs.plone.org/contributing/documentation/index.html) and the [First-time contributors](https://6.docs.plone.org/contributing/first-time.html) guidelines.
- Explore the available resources related to Cookieplone to build a foundational understanding.
- Consider joining discussions or asking questions in the issue comments to clarify any uncertainties.

issues.title: Merge Admin and Deployment guides into Develop guide

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
## What this issue is about
This issue proposes merging the existing Admin and Deployment guides into a single Develop guide for Plone. The aim is to simplify navigation for users who may wear multiple hats—like Admin, DevOps, and Developer—by consolidating relevant tasks into one comprehensive resource.

## What needs to be done
You will need to:
1. Review the current Admin and Deployment guides.
2. Identify overlapping content and tasks that can be combined.
3. Create a new Develop guide that includes all necessary information for site administration and development in one place.

## Helpful background
Previous discussions indicated that splitting tasks by role makes it harder for users to find what they need. A "Plone site administrator" is defined separately from an "Administrator," which has caused some confusion. This merging seeks to clarify roles and make the content more accessible.

## Good first steps
1. Familiarize yourself with the existing guides by reviewing them directly.
2. Check related issues and discussions linked [here](https://github.com/plone/documentation/issues/1768) and [here](https://github.com/plone/documentation/pull/1875).
3. Start drafting a combined outline for the new Develop guide.
4. Share your progress with the community for feedback!

issues.title: One File-Field (Type) to rules them all

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
## What this issue is about
This issue proposes consolidating Plone's file-handling system to use a single field type for all file types, rather than separating them into specific types like bitmap images and other files. This change aims to simplify content management and enhance user experience by allowing better file differentiation based on MIME type.

## What needs to be done
The main tasks include:
- Deprecating specialized fields like `NamedImage` and `NamedBlobImage`.
- Implementing a unified field that handles all file types while providing the necessary functionality based on their MIME types.
- Ensuring backward compatibility and assessing potential risks associated with this change.

## Helpful background
This enhancement stems from the recognition that current file handling in Plone can be confusing for editors and users. The discussion indicates that while there's general support for this simplification, there are concerns about the implications for content types and indexing files. This needs careful consideration.

## Good first steps
1. Familiarize yourself with Plone's file handling and the existing field types.
2. Review the related comments in the issue for insights and opinions for and against the proposal.
3. Get involved in discussions on possible side effects and share your thoughts in the comments for better clarity.
4. Prepare a simple example implementation to demonstrate how this change could work.

issues.title: Link Widget Improvement (add/select/modify)

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
```markdown
## What this issue is about
This issue (PLIP 4303) focuses on improving the user experience of adding links to internal content or external URLs in Plone. The goal is to unify the link-adding process across different components, providing a more seamless experience akin to those seen in other popular platforms.

## What needs to be done
We need to design a new link widget with the following features:
- Define the default elements to display (e.g., recent user-created content).
- Implement inline searching for links.
- Include options to save, edit, and remove links.
- Address any edge cases that may arise.

## Helpful background
This is a proposed improvement, not a bug to fix. It requires discussion, review, and approval from the project team. The idea has gained traction due to its relevance and appeal in modern software such as Google products and WordPress.

## Good first steps
```

issues.title: Refactor `plone.app.contentrules` to provide API methods, which `plone.restapi` can use

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
```markdown
## What this issue is about
This issue involves refactoring `plone.app.contentrules` to create Python API methods that can be utilized by `plone.restapi`. The goal is to separate UI-related code from the API, improving maintainability and allowing headless installations to function seamlessly.

## What needs to be done
1. Develop Python API methods for all existing functionality in `plone.app.contentrules`.
2. Move UI code to `plone.app.layout`.
3. Update `plone.restapi` to use these new API methods.

## Helpful background
Currently, `plone.restapi` fills form data directly, which ties it to the UI code in `plone.app.contentrules`. This refactor aims to decouple those dependencies. Although there are remarks about possibly turning `plone.app.contentrules` into a core add-on, this issue primarily focuses on the API refactoring.

## Good first steps
1. Familiarize yourself with the existing `plone.app.contentrules` and `plone.restapi` code.
2. Explore how Python API methods can be structured.
3. Engage in discussions about the feasibility of separating UI from API, as there is some ambiguity in the ongoing conversation regarding this refactor.
4. Consider getting involved in related issues (like PLIP #4119) for broader context.
5. Look out for testing and documentation needs once implementation is underway.
```

issues.title: Support for Subpath Domains

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
```markdown
## What this issue is about
This issue focuses on improving Volto to support multiple sites under one domain using subpath URLs, like `www.mysite.com/foo`. This feature aims to simplify project setups that need to host different sites within the same domain while keeping a clean and consistent URL structure.

## What needs to be done
To achieve this, we require:
- The Plone site root must operate under `./foo`.
- Build assets should be served from a designated environment variable, `RAZZLE_PREFIX_PATH`.
- All images and links must be prefixed accordingly.
- Update API requests to strip the `/foo` path.
- Configure Nginx for deployment, along with necessary documentation and Cypress tests.

## Helpful background
The concept is based on improved routing in React applications. This implementation will help manage routing and assets more efficiently, reducing manual configuration. There are several proposed methods, including the use of middleware and updated React components for links and images.

## Good first steps
1. Familiarize yourself with the existing PRs related to this feature, focusing on [PR #3592](https://github.com/plone/volto/pull/3592) and the discussion around its implementation.
2. Consider reviewing any related issues or PRs to grasp the current state of development.
3. Reach out to the contributors mentioned for guidance or collaboration, especially regarding documentation and testing strategies.
```

issues.title: `@plone/contents` - Enhance Contents View

This issue focuses on enhancing the `Contents` view in `@plone/contents` to improve usability and accessibility. The goal is to refactor the contents view, making it a mini-app that operates independently from the site frame, improving overall user experience. 

## What needs to be done
- Refactor the `Contents.jsx` component to utilize the `@plone/contents` core package.
- Implement better accessibility features using `react-aria-components`.
- Avoid using SemanticUI and instead adopt TypeScript and better component slicing for maintainability.

## Good first steps

Testing and documentation updates will be expected after implementation. Happy contributing!

issues.title: Proposal: have a list of "smart fields" in Volto blocks

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
## What this issue is about
This issue proposes creating a list of "smart fields" for Volto blocks. These fields would be automatically processed on the server side, enhancing functionality and simplifying block management. Examples include fields like `searchableText` and `relatedItems`.

## What needs to be done
We need to:
1. Define which fields will be considered "smart fields."
2. Implement the necessary logic for server-side processing of these fields.
3. Document the smart fields thoroughly so block authors understand their potential side-effects.

## Helpful background
"Smart fields" are special fields in Volto blocks that have additional behaviors, like automatic indexing or content resolution. For instance, using a `url` field could directly leverage existing code to resolve item references. There may be competing ideas or requirements in this discussion, so clarity on final decisions is essential.

## Good first steps
1. Review current comments and suggestions in the issue to gather ideas on potential smart fields.
2. Begin drafting a list of these fields and their intended behaviors.
3. Familiarize yourself with how existing fields function to understand common patterns.
4. Once a preliminary list is ready, contribute to the documentation for clarity.

issues.title: Quanta Toolbar

issues.title: Control panels

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
```markdown
## What this issue is about
This issue focuses on creating various control panels for the Plone Volto project. Control panels allow for configuration and management of different aspects of Plone. Currently, many REST API calls needed for these panels are not yet available.

## What needs to be done
Several control panels need to be developed, including:
- Addons
- Discussion
- Syndication
- Content settings
- HTML filtering
- Configuration Registry

Some panels, like "Dexterity content types" and "Users and groups," have been discussed but lack complete implementation. The approach will possibly involve creating REST API endpoints first, followed by the frontend implementation.

## Helpful background
Control panels in Plone help manage functionalities through a user-friendly interface. The development process may require a good understanding of the Plone framework and REST APIs. Be aware that there might still be some uncertainty regarding the exact structure and requirements for these control panels.

## Good first steps
```

issues.title: Draft: Plone Multi Factor Authentication

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
## What this issue is about
This issue focuses on implementing Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) for Plone to enhance security. The aim is to support several MFA methods, including OTOP (Authenticator Apps), FIDO2 hardware tokens, and Passkeys, while offering integration with Single Sign-On (SSO) systems.

## What needs to be done
We need to design and implement various MFA schemes. This will involve creating a control panel in Plone for configuring MFA requirements, where users can select settings based on their roles (e.g., optional, enforced for admins, or for all users). The implementation will include both backend and frontend components for Plone's different user interfaces.

## Helpful background
There’s ongoing discussion about potentially breaking this large task into smaller, manageable pieces (PLIPs) to make it easier to implement individual MFA systems. UX design considerations are also essential for a seamless experience when integrating the different MFA methods.

## Good first steps
1. **Familiarize yourself** with Plone's [contributing guidelines](https://6.docs.plone.org/contributing/index.html).
2. **Read through existing discussions** in this issue about UX design and breakdown suggestions to understand the scope and complexity.
3. Consider participating in discussions to help clarify requirements or suggest improvements based on user experience.

issues.title: PLIP Process for User Interfacing Changing PLIPs

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
## What this issue is about
This issue focuses on improving the PLIP (Plone Improvement Proposal) process for user interface (UI) and user experience (UX) changes in Volto (Plone's frontend framework). Changes in this area can often be breaking and may require careful planning and expert input.

## What needs to be done
1. Identify PLIPs that propose UI/UX changes.
2. Collaborate with UX experts through meetings and sprints to develop solutions.
3. Implement the agreed-upon proposals and submit for review by the Volto team.

## Helpful background
The discussion highlights a current challenge: limited UX participation within the Volto team and community. Suggestions include reaching out directly to UX professionals and facilitating their involvement in the PLIP process. There's also a mention of creating a forum or tagging system for better engagement and feedback.

issues.title: json_body should not read entire request BODY

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
```markdown
## What this issue is about
This issue addresses a bug in `plone.restapi` where the `json_body` function reads the entire request body into memory, causing failures with requests larger than 1MB in Zope 5.8.4+. It can lead to high memory usage and potential denial-of-service threats.

## What needs to be done
We need to modify the `json_body` function to avoid reading the full body into memory. Instead, we should explore methods to handle larger data streams effectively and safely. Discussions suggest investigating alternatives like using a streaming approach or changing the serialization format for better handling of uploads.

## Helpful background
The issue is tied to environmental limits on memory and has sparked discussions about optimal handling of JSON data in Plone. Temporary fixes, such as adjusting limits in `zope.conf`, have been implemented but are not sustainable.

## Good first steps
1. Review the current `json_body` implementation and consider alternative approaches, like using the `ijson` library for streaming JSON parsing.
2. Check how `json_body` is utilized in the codebase to understand the potential impact of changes.
3. Engage with other contributors in the issue's comments to gather insights and align on potential solutions.
4. Write tests to ensure the new implementation meets functionality and safety requirements.
```

issues.title: Unable to edit frontpage in Volto site without "Manage portal" permission

```markdown
## What this issue is about
This issue addresses a limitation in Volto where users need the "Manage portal" permission to edit the front page of a Plone site. This requirement seems overly strict, given that the front page isn't a standard content type within Plone.

## What needs to be done
The goal is to explore a more user-friendly way to handle editing permissions for the front page. This may involve enhancing how the "layout" property from Plone interacts with Volto's content serialization process.

## Helpful background
Currently, Volto doesn't treat the Plone site root like typical content, which complicates editing. A proposed solution involves modifying the ContentGet service to serialize additional content linked to the "default_page" property, allowing it to be displayed alongside the current content.

## Good first steps
```

issues.title: Script: remove `pkg_resources` namespace declarations

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
```markdown
## What this issue is about
This issue addresses the need to remove `pkg_resources` namespace declarations from `__init__.py` files in the Plone codebase. As Plone evolves, particularly towards version 7, transitioning to native namespaces would enhance compatibility and performance. 

## What needs to be done
We should develop a script to automate the removal of these deprecated namespace declarations. The goal is to ensure a smoother transition to native namespaces as outlined in the Python Packaging documentation.

## Helpful background
Currently, both old and new namespace package styles can coexist, but using `pkg_resources` is outdated and can complicate editable installs. As per the discussions, it's recommended to remove the namespace-level `__init__.py` files since they are no longer necessary following PEP 420 guidelines.

## Good first steps
1. Familiarize yourself with the [Python Packaging guidelines](https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/guides/packaging-namespace-packages#native-namespace-packages).
2. Review the current `__init__.py` files in the Plone repository.
3. Consider contributing to the script's development by proposing a design or a draft.
4. Join discussions with project maintainers for coordination, particularly regarding testing and release strategies.
```

issues.title: Do releases with `tox`

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
## What this issue is about
This issue proposes automating the release process for the Plone package using `tox`. The goal is to utilize GitHub Actions to generate package archives and publish them to PyPI securely.

## What needs to be done
1. Set up GitHub Actions to:
   - Generate `sdist` and `wheel` archives using the [build-and-inspect-python-package](https://github.com/hynek/build-and-inspect-python-package) action.
   - Use the [gh-action-pypi-publish](https://github.com/pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish) for publishing the archives to PyPI.
2. Integrate the "trusted publishing" feature on PyPI to keep credentials secure.
3. Implement a pre-action to update the change log via `towncrier`.
4. Implement a post-action to update `versions.cfg` and `checkouts.cfg`.

## Helpful background
While the issue has a clear foundational plan, the pre- and post-actions may need further detail. The discussion also hints at potential triggers for the release workflow but lacks definitive consensus on the overall execution.

## Good first steps
1. Familiarize yourself with the mentioned GitHub Actions and how `tox` integrates with the Plone setup.
2. Explore the `towncrier` tool for updating change logs.
3. Review the "trusted publishers" feature on PyPI to understand its benefits.
4. Consider discussing the issue further to clarify any uncertainties around implementation.

issues.title: Add a GHA to create Github  releases

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
```markdown
## What this issue is about
This issue proposes adding a GitHub Action (GHA) to automatically create GitHub releases from tags. Releases are useful for marketing and allow users to subscribe for notifications on updates.

## What needs to be done
You'll need to implement a GHA that gathers necessary release information and makes a GitHub API call to create the release using the `GITHUB_TOKEN`. The current codebase may offer insights, especially from a related repository, `scriv`.

## Helpful background
Creating a release involves using the GitHub API. Discussions suggest that while the initial approach seems straightforward, there are complexities in managing multiline data in API calls, especially when dealing with changelogs from `CHANGES.rst` or `CHANGES.md`.

There are ongoing conversations about handling cases when `[ci skip]` is used in commits, which currently prevents the GHA from triggering.

## Good first steps
1. Familiarize yourself with GitHub Actions and the GitHub REST API for releases.
2. Review the relevant code in the `scriv` repository for examples.
3. Set up a local environment to test your implementation.
4. Be prepared to manage any issues that arise with changelog formats or the `[ci skip]` limitation.
5. Consider contributing to ongoing discussions to streamline the release process further.
```

issues.title: enforce layers split

```markdown
## What this issue is about
This issue discusses the need to enforce a clear separation of layers in our codebase as we transition some addons to core-addons. The goal is to limit the use of `plone.api` in certain areas while allowing it in others. To assist with this, the tool **import-linter** may be utilized for automated checks.

## What needs to be done
We aim to implement **import-linter** to verify that the correct packages are used within the designated layers. While this discussion is optimistic about using the tool, specific implementation steps, and whether all considerations are covered, still need clarification.

## Helpful background
The tool **import-linter** helps manage dependencies and can check both internal and external packages. Some conversations have highlighted its potential beyond just checking imports, but further ideas on additional functionality remain vague.

## Good first steps
1. Explore the [import-linter documentation](https://import-linter.readthedocs.io/en/stable/index.html) to understand its capabilities.
2. Review the existing issue comments for insights and further discussion links.
3. Consider discussing with team members like [gforcada](https://github.com/gforcada) and [jensens](https://github.com/jensens) regarding specific requirements.
4. Try setting up **import-linter** in a test environment to see how it operates with our codebase.
```

issues.title: Autocomplete widget not normalizing on search

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
# Autocomplete widget not normalizing on search

## What this issue is about
This issue addresses a problem with the autocomplete widget in Plone that fails to recognize names with accents. For example, typing "hec..." does not suggest "Héctor Velarde" due to the lack of normalization of accented characters.

## What needs to be done
We need to enhance the autocomplete widget to ensure it can return suggestions regardless of accent marks or character variations. This may involve improving how data is indexed and searched within the component.

## Helpful background
Currently, it seems that the widget uses a simple keyword index that does not normalize strings. Normalization is the process of converting different forms of a character to a standard form (e.g., treating "é" and "e" as equivalent). The discussion indicates some uncertainty around existing implementations and their limitations.

## Good first steps
1. Familiarize yourself with the autocomplete widget in Plone, especially focusing on its indexing mechanism.
2. Review how normalization is handled in similar components or libraries.
3. Consider checking existing tests related to the widget and see if enhancements are needed or can be added.
4. Discuss your findings or proposed changes in the issue comments for feedback before diving into coding.

issues.title: Copy&Paste feature overlaps with DX Layout restrictions

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
## What this issue is about
This issue discusses a problem where the copy and paste feature allows users to bypass layout restrictions meant to prevent the creation of new blocks in Plone's Volto. It suggests a need for a way to limit this feature at the block level.

## What needs to be done
We need to develop a method to disable the copy and paste functionality for specific blocks. This will involve creating rules around how these restrictions should work and providing an API for implementation.

## Helpful background
Currently, there is some built-in functionality in the `pasteBlocks` method, where blocks can define a `cloneData` function in their configuration. However, it might not be sufficient to enforce copy restrictions effectively. Clear rules must be established for this feature, as the existing discussion indicates some ambiguity around implementation details.

## Good first steps
1. Review the `pasteBlocks` function and the `cloneData` implementation in the codebase.
2. Start thinking about what rules should govern the copy/paste functionality for blocks.
3. Consider drafting a potential API design that could expose these rules for reuse in the platform.
4. Engage with the community for feedback on your ideas and check for any existing discussions that might help clarify the requirements.

issues.title: adopt_roles does not work as I expected

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
## What this issue is about
This issue revolves around the `api.env.adopt_roles` function in Plone, which is not working as expected. Users anticipated it would either temporarily grant roles during a code block or use a proxy user with those roles. However, current observations show it doesn't seem to alter permissions as expected.

## What needs to be done
We need to clarify the intended behavior of `adopt_roles`. If it’s confirmed that the current functionality is a bug, it should be addressed in the code. Additionally, the documentation needs updating to explain what `adopt_roles` actually does.

## Helpful background
Multiple contributors have raised similar issues. They noticed that while granting roles directly using `api.user.grant_roles` operates effectively, `adopt_roles` fails to provide the expected permissions, particularly in the same request context. There's speculation that caching may play a role in this behavior.

## Good first steps
1. Review the existing tests in the mentioned [link](https://github.com/plone/plone.api/blob/master/src/plone/api/tests/test_env.py#L134-L174) to understand current expectations.
2. Test the behavior of `adopt_roles` in simple scenarios, noting where it fails to grant access.
3. If issues persist, contribute by updating documentation to specify the behavior of `adopt_roles`.
4. Coordinate with other contributors who are interested in resolving these issues for collaborative debugging and solutions.

issues.title: Encourage usage of XPath in rules.xml

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
```markdown
## What this issue is about
This issue aims to improve performance in the `rules.xml` of `bobtemplates.plone` by encouraging the use of XPath with absolute selectors instead of CSS selectors. The motivation is based on recent experiences showing that CSS selectors can lead to slow, global queries.

## What needs to be done
To enhance performance, we should replace existing CSS selectors with more efficient XPath queries. The specific examples discussed involve changing rules for the `#visual-portal-wrapper`, as outlined by contributors, though there is some disagreement on whether XPath should be the standard approach.

## Helpful background
The issue of performance relates to how many global searches are triggered by CSS selectors, which can be costly. XPath can be more explicit and often results in faster queries, particularly on longer pages. However, some contributors have suggested alternatives, and there is no consensus on making XPath the preferred method.

## Good first steps
- Review the current `rules.xml` file in `bobtemplates.plone`.
- Identify instances where CSS selectors are used for `#visual-portal-wrapper`.
- Experiment with replacing those with the recommended XPath examples.
- Consider discussing findings with other contributors for feedback and possible adjustments.
- Familiarize yourself with XPath if you are new to it, as it's a bit more complex than CSS selectors but quite powerful.
```

issues.title: Collaborative editing

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
## What this issue is about
This issue discusses the enhancement of adding collaborative editing features to the Plone Volto application. The goal is to allow multiple users to edit content simultaneously, enhancing teamwork and improving user experience.

## What needs to be done
We need to implement collaborative editing functionality, utilizing an editor framework that supports document structure as a tree, which can be compared (diffed). It will involve extending the current editor features, like images and links, based on the existing medium-like styles.

## Helpful background
Collaborative editing allows multiple people to work on the same document at the same time. This specific enhancement leverages an editing framework that saves the editor state in an immutable object. The current Volto editor supports block and inline styles, which can be expanded to include more features.

## Good first steps
1. Familiarize yourself with the current editor used in Volto.
2. Review existing discussions and ideas around collaborative editing in this issue.
3. Consider how the proposed features can integrate with the current structure.
4. If you're unfamiliar with certain terms or technologies mentioned, don’t hesitate to ask for clarification in the comments!
5. Coordinate with existing contributors for feedback on your ideas.

issues.title: RegistryReader has bad performance on sites with a lot of users

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
# Summary of Issue #66: RegistryReader Performance

## What this issue is about
This issue addresses poor performance in the `RegistryReader` for Plone sites with many users. Specifically, the `@@qsOptions` view takes too long to generate options for query fields when many users and large vocabularies are present, particularly affecting sites integrated with LDAP.

## What needs to be done
The objective is to improve the performance of the `@@qsOptions` view, which retrieves queryable fields and their distinct values from the registry. There are currently suggestions for quick local fixes, although a long-term solution is needed.

## Helpful background
The current implementation fetches all distinct field values, which can be overwhelming for sites with numerous users. Suggested fixes include modifying registry values to limit the options returned. Note that the conversation includes various approaches, and a definitive solution has yet to be agreed upon.

## Good first steps
1. Review the existing comments for potential quick fixes, such as updating registry field values.
2. Test these local fixes and evaluate their impact on performance.
3. Consider participating in discussions around alternative solutions or contributing your own ideas.
4. Be prepared to run tests and document any changes or improvements you suggest. 

Feel free to reach out if you have questions or need assistance!

issues.title: pre-commit hook should only be run on modified files

contributor_summary.summary_markdown:
## What this issue is about
This issue addresses an enhancement for the `plone.recipe.codeanalysis` repository. The goal is to modify the pre-commit hook so that it runs only on files that have been modified, rather than on the entire codebase. This change aims to reduce unnecessary validations, making the process more efficient.

## What needs to be done
The pre-commit hook should be updated to check which files have been modified using the command:
```
git diff --cached --name-only
```
This will allow tools like Flake8, JSHint, and CSS Lint to run only on the relevant files (Python, JavaScript, and CSS respectively). Discussion around whether to retain the old way as an option is still ongoing.

## Helpful background
Currently, when committing changes, the pre-commit hook runs validations on all files, which can be inefficient. Enhancing it to target modified files could streamline the development process. A related pull request has been created to introduce this filtering.

## Good first steps
1. Familiarize yourself with the existing pre-commit hook code in the repository.
2. Review the command `git diff --cached --name-only` to understand how it identifies changed files.
3. Consider the implications of keeping the old behavior as an option.
4. Test your changes thoroughly to ensure the hook still runs appropriate checks.
